notes: Simulation always starts at user's current age from backend (user_age in AsyncStorage)
Pre-existing conditions read from user_conditions in AsyncStorage
Relationship content is opt-in via settings toggle (relationship_content_enabled in AsyncStorage)
Health sidebar rotates through user's conditions one per age — index tracked in simulation_condition_index
Family planning track has 4 age brackets with different content and framing
Condition list: pcos, pcod, endometriosis, fibroids, thyroid, iron_deficiency, calcium_vitamin_d

first_ask.prompt: Some of LEA's scenarios touch on relationships — romantic partnerships, cohabitation, life planning with a partner. Would you like to include relationship-related content in your simulation?
first_ask.no: Sets relationship_content_enabled: false in AsyncStorage. Relationship decisions are replaced with a second career or health decision for that age.
first_ask.shown_at: Start of simulation, before age 21 content loads

re_enable.location: Settings toggle labelled: Include relationship scenarios in my simulation
re_enable.behaviour: Sets relationship_content_enabled: true and resumes from next age

_condition_rotation_logic.description: At each age, health sidebar shows content for one condition from user's condition list. Index increments each age, wrapping back to 0 after last condition. If user has no conditions, default sidebar is shown with proactive screening prompts.
_condition_rotation_logic.example: User has [pcos, iron_deficiency]. Age 21 shows pcos sidebar. Age 22 shows iron_deficiency sidebar. Age 23 shows pcos sidebar again.
_condition_rotation_logic.multiple_condition_note: Each sidebar acknowledges the user's full condition list in passing but focuses on the rotating condition in depth.

_family_planning_brackets.21-23: Early awareness — introducing the concept that family planning is a spectrum of options, not a deadline. Very low pressure. Focus on education.
_family_planning_brackets.24-27: Active consideration window — realistic timeline information, what planning alongside a career looks like, pre-conception health.
_family_planning_brackets.28-31: Decision window — practical information about trying to conceive, fertility preservation, maternity leave, career timing.
_family_planning_brackets.32-35: Extended options window — honest information about natural conception rates, assisted reproduction, adoption as equally valid paths. No fear, just facts.

decisions.narrative: You're either finishing your degree or stepping into your first real job. The world is genuinely open — and that openness is both exciting and disorienting. Lea sits beside you, patient.

choice_a.label: Dive straight into work — get ahead while others are still figuring it out
choice_a.consequence: You hit the ground running. The learning curve was steep and the experience was real. You're already ahead of where you expected to be.

choice_b.label: Take time to explore first — travel, internships, figure out what you want
choice_b.consequence: The exploration gave you clarity that rushing wouldn't have. You entered the workforce later but with intention.

decisions.narrative: Health has never been something you've thought about much — that's been your parents' domain. But you're on your own now, and your body is yours to understand.

choice_a.label: Start paying attention — book a first adult health checkup
choice_a.consequence: The checkup was unremarkable and useful. You have a baseline now. That reference point will matter later.

choice_b.label: I'm young and healthy — I'll deal with health stuff when it becomes relevant
choice_b.consequence: Nothing dramatic has happened. But you also don't know what your normal looks like — which means you won't notice when it changes.

decisions.narrative: Your first serious adult relationship is either underway or on the horizon. The stakes feel higher than before — because they are.

choice_a.label: Invest fully — this relationship deserves real attention
choice_a.consequence: You showed up. The relationship grew into something real — or it ended cleanly. Either way you were present for it.

choice_b.label: Keep it light — I'm not ready to prioritise a relationship right now
choice_b.consequence: You kept your freedom. You learned what you actually want from a relationship by not forcing one.

default.headline: Your body at 21 — what's worth knowing now
default.body: This is the most important window for bone density. Calcium and Vitamin D absorbed in your early twenties have a 30-year structural payoff — your bones stop building mass by your early thirties. Your cycle, if it's been irregular through school and exam stress, may still be settling. This is worth logging rather than ignoring.
default.action: Check whether you've completed your HPV vaccine series — recommended before 26 in Singapore and available at polyclinics.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 21 — this is the right time to establish your baseline
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, your early twenties are the most important window for establishing a management baseline. PCOS is a metabolic condition as much as a hormonal one — insulin resistance, androgen levels, and cycle regularity all interact. What you understand and manage now shapes your long-term metabolic health significantly.
pcos.action: Book a PCOS management review with your GP. Ask for a full panel: androgens, insulin, LH/FSH ratio, and a pelvic ultrasound if you haven't had one recently.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 21 — lifestyle is your most powerful tool right now
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, the good news is that at 21 your condition responds strongly to lifestyle intervention. Diet, regular movement, and stress management can significantly reduce symptoms and prevent progression to PCOS. This is the most impactful window for those changes.
pcod.action: Ask your GP whether your current management approach is optimised for your age. Diet and exercise referrals are available through most polyclinics in Singapore.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 21 — don't let it keep being dismissed
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, you already know how long the road to diagnosis can be. At 21, establishing a specialist relationship is the most important thing you can do. A gynaecologist who takes your condition seriously, rather than managing it at GP level, changes your long-term trajectory.
endometriosis.action: If you don't already have a gynaecologist managing your endometriosis, ask your GP for a referral. In Singapore, KKH and NUH both have dedicated endometriosis services.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 21 — monitoring is the priority right now
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, at 21 the focus is on monitoring growth and managing any symptoms — particularly heavy bleeding that can lead to iron deficiency. Most fibroids at this age are small and asymptomatic, but knowing their size and location gives you a baseline for comparison over time.
fibroids.action: Ensure you have a recent pelvic ultrasound on record — within the last 12 months — so you have a size and location baseline for your fibroids.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 21 — optimal management matters now
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, the demands of early adult life — irregular sleep, dietary changes, stress — can affect thyroid function significantly. Annual TSH monitoring is the standard but 'within normal range' may not mean optimal for you. Fatigue, weight changes, or mood shifts that have returned since your last review are worth flagging.
thyroid.action: If your last thyroid review was more than 12 months ago or your symptoms have changed, book a review rather than waiting for your scheduled test.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 21 — building your levels while you can
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, your early twenties are when the pattern of monthly blood loss begins compounding against your stores most significantly. Dietary iron, absorption with Vitamin C, and avoiding calcium-rich foods with iron supplements are the first-line interventions. Many women need supplementation throughout their reproductive years.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP to recheck your ferritin level and discuss whether your current supplementation approach is adequate. Ferritin above 30 is the target — not just haemoglobin within range.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 21 — the investment with the longest payoff
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, you are missing the single most important bone-building window of your life. The bone density you build before your late twenties is what you live on for the rest of your life. Correcting this now has a payoff that extends 50 years.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm with your GP that your current supplementation is achieving target levels — not just supplementing. A follow-up Vitamin D blood test 3 months after starting supplementation is standard to confirm correction.

track_prompt.prompt: Family planning means different things at different ages — from understanding your options to active planning. At 21, it might just mean knowing what's out there. Want to explore what that looks like?

decisions.narrative: You've graduated or you've had your first taste of real work. Two paths have emerged — and both are legitimate.

choice_a.label: Join a high-growth startup — fast learning, high risk, high reward
choice_a.consequence: The startup was everything it promised — chaotic, educational, and exhausting. You've grown faster than you expected and aged faster than you'd like.

choice_b.label: Take a structured graduate program at a large company
choice_b.consequence: The program gave you foundations. You understand how organisations actually work. Now you're asking whether you want to work inside one forever.

decisions.narrative: You've been tired in a way that feels different from just being busy. A colleague mentioned she felt the same until she got a blood test and found out she was iron deficient.

choice_a.label: Get a blood test — rule out anything physical before blaming it on lifestyle
choice_a.consequence: Low ferritin. You've been iron deficient probably for longer than you realised. You're treating it now and the difference is noticeable.

choice_b.label: It's just the adjustment — push through
choice_b.consequence: The tiredness has become your baseline. You've stopped questioning it. That normalisation is worth examining.

decisions.narrative: Someone in your life is asking for more commitment — time, energy, future plans. It's the first time a relationship has felt genuinely weighty.

choice_a.label: Give it what it needs — this is worth it
choice_a.consequence: You chose to invest and the relationship grew into something real. You've learned things about yourself that only come from commitment.

choice_b.label: Not ready to prioritise this right now
choice_b.consequence: You were honest about where you are. The relationship either adjusted or ended. Both were acceptable outcomes.

default.headline: Iron deficiency — the condition hiding as a busy lifestyle
default.body: Women lose iron monthly through menstruation and it is one of the most under-recognised deficiencies in young women. The symptoms — fatigue, difficulty concentrating, low mood, feeling cold — are identical to the experience of a demanding early career. A ferritin test takes one appointment to rule out.
default.action: Ask for ferritin specifically at your next blood test. A standard full blood count doesn't always catch low iron stores before they become clinical anaemia.

pcos.headline: PCOS and work stress — a combination worth monitoring
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, the chronic stress of early career life elevates cortisol, which worsens insulin resistance — a core mechanism in your condition. High-pressure work periods can trigger irregular cycles, energy crashes, and skin changes. This is not a reason to avoid ambition. It is a reason to monitor your symptoms during demanding periods.
pcos.action: Track cycle regularity and energy levels during your highest-pressure work months. Bring patterns to your next GP or endocrinologist review.

pcod.headline: PCOD and the lifestyle window at 22
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, you are still in the window where lifestyle interventions have the strongest effect. Regular movement, reduced refined sugar, and stress management can significantly reduce immature follicle accumulation. The habits built at 22 have a disproportionate impact on how PCOD progresses.
pcod.action: If you haven't had a follow-up ultrasound since diagnosis, book one. Seeing whether cyst burden has changed with lifestyle gives you concrete feedback.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis and the work environment at 22
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, managing your condition in a work environment requires self-advocacy that isn't always easy. Knowing your rights around medical leave, managing pain during flares, and having a clear pain management plan are practical necessities at this stage — not luxuries.
endometriosis.action: Ensure you have a current pain management plan from your gynaecologist that covers what to do during a severe flare at work, not just routine management.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids and iron deficiency — a common pairing at 22
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, heavy menstrual bleeding is one of the most common symptoms — and it is also a leading cause of iron deficiency in women your age. If you feel consistently tired and have heavy periods, these two things are likely connected and both are worth addressing.
fibroids.action: Ask your GP to check your ferritin level alongside your next fibroid monitoring ultrasound. Heavy periods plus fibroids plus fatigue is a clinical pattern, not bad luck.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid and fatigue at 22 — don't let it be normalised
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, fatigue that doesn't resolve with adequate sleep is a specific symptom worth flagging rather than tolerating. In the early career years it's easy to attribute everything to stress. If your energy is significantly lower than your peers in similar circumstances, your thyroid management may need reviewing.
thyroid.action: If your energy levels haven't improved despite adequate sleep and nutrition, book a thyroid review rather than waiting for your next scheduled check.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 22 — checking your treatment is working
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, treatment needs follow-up — supplementing without retesting doesn't tell you whether your levels have actually corrected. Ferritin above 30 micrograms per litre is the target. Many women supplement for months without reaching this because absorption varies significantly.
iron_deficiency.action: If you've been supplementing for 3 months or more, ask for a ferritin retest to confirm your levels have corrected. If they haven't, absorption may be the issue — IV iron is an option.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 22 — the follow-up matters
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, the supplement alone is only the first step. Vitamin D absorption varies significantly between people and the only way to know whether your supplementation is working is a follow-up blood test. Many people supplement for years without reaching adequate levels.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Book a Vitamin D follow-up test if you haven't had one since starting supplementation. Target level is 75 nmol/L or above — not just out of the deficient range.

decisions.narrative: A year in and you're being properly evaluated for the first time. The performance review is real. So is the gap between people who are climbing and people who are coasting.

choice_a.label: Go all in — late nights, high visibility, make your mark early
choice_a.consequence: Your name is known. You delivered results. You also haven't slept properly in months and your body is registering the cost in ways you're starting to notice.

choice_b.label: Perform well but protect your limits — sustainable is the strategy
choice_b.consequence: You delivered consistently without burning out. Some peers moved faster. You moved steadier — and you still feel like yourself.

decisions.narrative: Your periods have been irregular for over a year. You've attributed it to stress. A friend was recently diagnosed with PCOS after dismissing the same pattern for years.

choice_a.label: Investigate it — irregular cycles for over a year warrant a conversation
choice_a.consequence: You pushed for an answer. Whether the result was a diagnosis or a clear all-clear, you now know. The uncertainty was worse.

choice_b.label: It's probably stress — I'll see if it settles
choice_b.consequence: Another year has passed. The irregularity hasn't resolved. You're still attributing it to stress without knowing whether that's true.

decisions.narrative: You're starting to understand your own patterns in relationships — what you bring, what you need, what you keep getting wrong.

choice_a.label: Do the work — understanding your patterns is worth the discomfort
choice_a.consequence: The self-examination was uncomfortable and useful. You entered your next relationship with more self-awareness than you'd had before.

choice_b.label: Not right now — I'll figure this out as I go
choice_b.consequence: You kept moving. The patterns are still there. They'll show up again.

default.headline: PCOS — peak undiagnosed age is right now
default.body: PCOS affects 1 in 10 women of reproductive age and up to 70 percent are undiagnosed. Your early-to-mid twenties are when symptoms become consistent enough to investigate clearly. Irregular cycles, acne, unexplained weight changes, or hair thinning are the most common signals. Early diagnosis meaningfully reduces long-term metabolic risk regardless of fertility plans.
default.action: If your cycles have been irregular for more than 12 months, ask your GP for a PCOS screening panel: pelvic ultrasound, androgens, insulin, and LH/FSH ratio.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 23 — are you getting the right monitoring?
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, by 23 you should have an established annual review rhythm. This covers your hormonal panel, metabolic markers, and cycle patterns. If your management has been reactive — treating symptoms as they arise — rather than proactive, now is a good time to shift that.
pcos.action: Ask your GP or endocrinologist for a proactive management plan, not just reactive symptom treatment. Annual metabolic monitoring is the standard of care.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 23 — have you been tested for PCOS progression?
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, it's worth checking whether your condition has progressed toward PCOS — particularly if you've had a stressful year or significant lifestyle changes. PCOD can transition to PCOS and the management approach shifts accordingly. A hormonal panel comparison against your baseline is how you track this.
pcod.action: Ask for a hormonal panel comparison against your previous results. If androgens or insulin have increased, your management approach may need updating.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 23 — is your pain management still working?
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, pain management that worked at 20 or 21 may not be adequate at 23 if your condition has progressed. Endometriosis is progressive in many cases. If your pain levels have increased or your current treatment is less effective than it was, a specialist review is warranted rather than dose escalation without assessment.
endometriosis.action: Rate your current pain management effectiveness honestly — is it working as well as it did 12 months ago? If not, request a specialist review rather than just increasing analgesia.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 23 — have you had a follow-up ultrasound?
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, monitoring growth is the primary concern at your age. Fibroids tend to grow slowly but the rate varies. A follow-up ultrasound 12 to 18 months after your baseline gives you a growth rate — which is more clinically useful than a single measurement.
fibroids.action: If it's been more than 12 months since your last pelvic ultrasound, book a follow-up to establish a growth rate for your fibroids.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 23 — stress affects your levels
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, sustained high-pressure periods can affect thyroid function and may require dose adjustment. If you've had a significantly stressful year and your symptoms — fatigue, weight, mood, hair — have shifted, your TSH may have moved even if you're not due for a scheduled check.
thyroid.action: If you've had a high-stress year and your symptoms have changed, request a thyroid check now rather than waiting for your next scheduled one.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 23 — are your levels actually corrected?
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, many women reach a technically normal haemoglobin level but still have low ferritin stores — which means symptoms persist despite 'normal' blood results. Ferritin is the storage form and the more clinically meaningful number for how you feel.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask specifically for ferritin in your next blood test, not just haemoglobin. Target is ferritin above 30 micrograms per litre.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 23 — the bone density window is closing
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, your mid-twenties are the tail end of the primary bone-building window. The calcium your bones absorb before your late twenties is the structural reserve you live on for the rest of your life. This is the most urgent decade for correction.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: If your Vitamin D has not been retested since starting supplementation, book a test now. Correction should be confirmed, not assumed.

track_prompt.prompt: Before we move forward — some women your age find it useful to understand what family planning options exist at this stage, even as something to know rather than act on. Want a quick overview?

decisions.narrative: You're competent now — and competence has made the question sharper. Is this the right direction? A lateral move has come up. It would mean starting over in some ways.

choice_a.label: Stay and go deeper — master what you're building
choice_a.consequence: You doubled down and became genuinely good at something specific. The depth is starting to open doors that breadth couldn't.

choice_b.label: Make the pivot — better to find out now than at 30
choice_b.consequence: The pivot was disorienting for six months. By the end of the year it felt right. The work finally feels like yours.

decisions.narrative: You've never had a proper gynaecology checkup. Cervical screening is recommended from 25 in Singapore — but a baseline exam before that makes sense. A friend just had her first one and said she wished she'd done it sooner.

choice_a.label: Book a first gynaecology checkup — establish a baseline
choice_a.consequence: The appointment was unremarkable and reassuring. You know your baseline now. That reference point matters.

choice_b.label: I'll start when cervical screening is due at 25
choice_b.consequence: You're waiting for the formal milestone. Nothing is wrong — but you also don't have a picture of where you are.

decisions.narrative: A relationship in your life is at a crossroads — either deepening or revealing it isn't the right fit. Both outcomes require honesty.

choice_a.label: Have the direct conversation — clarity is kinder than ambiguity
choice_a.consequence: The conversation changed things. You're clearer on what you need from relationships now than you've ever been.

choice_b.label: Let it develop at its own pace
choice_b.consequence: You gave it space. It either grew or quietly resolved. Either way it didn't end in a forced conclusion.

default.headline: First gynaecology checkup — what to expect and why it matters
default.body: A first gynaecological exam at 24 covers a pelvic assessment, discussion of cycle patterns, and any symptoms you've been normalising. Cervical screening starts at 25 in Singapore but a baseline exam before that gives your doctor context. Many conditions — fibroids, ovarian cysts, PCOS — are found incidentally during routine exams in women with no symptoms.
default.action: Book a first gynaecology appointment at your polyclinic or a women's health clinic. Bring a 3-month cycle log if you have one.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 24 — reviewing your management as your life changes
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, life changes at 24 — career shifts, dietary changes, different stress patterns — affect your condition. Management that was right at 21 may not be optimal now. An annual review that looks at whether your current approach is still the right one is more useful than just repeating the same tests.
pcos.action: Ask for a management review rather than just a monitoring appointment. Ask specifically: is what I'm doing still the best approach for my age and current lifestyle?

pcod.headline: PCOD at 24 — checking for progression
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, a check for whether your condition has remained stable or progressed is appropriate at 24. The presence of irregular cycles, new hormonal symptoms, or worsening acne can indicate progression toward PCOS. Catching this early means the management shift happens proactively.
pcod.action: Ask for a comparison of your current hormonal panel against your earliest baseline. Progression is measurable if you have the data to compare.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 24 — are you getting specialist-level care?
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, GP-level management is often insufficient for the complexity of the condition. If your pain, fatigue, or quality of life impact is not well-controlled, a specialist review at a dedicated endometriosis service is the appropriate next step — not another round of the same treatment.
endometriosis.action: If you're not already under specialist care, ask your GP for a referral to a gynaecologist with endometriosis expertise. In Singapore, KKH and NUH have dedicated services.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 24 — monitoring and symptom management
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, at 24 the primary concerns are growth monitoring and symptom management — particularly if heavy bleeding is affecting your iron levels and quality of life. Treatment options exist beyond 'wait and see' if symptoms are significantly affecting you.
fibroids.action: At your next monitoring ultrasound, ask your doctor to discuss treatment options if symptoms are significantly impacting your quality of life — not just confirm size and location.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid at 24 — optimal vs normal
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, there is a meaningful difference between TSH within the broad normal range and TSH at the optimal level for your age and symptoms. Many women are managed to normal rather than optimal and continue to experience symptoms unnecessarily. This is worth a specific conversation with your doctor.
thyroid.action: Ask your GP specifically: what is my target TSH range, and is my current level at the optimal end of that range or just within it?

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 24 — dietary sources alongside supplementation
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, long-term supplementation is more sustainable when combined with dietary iron. Red meat, legumes, dark leafy greens, and fortified cereals all contribute. Vitamin C with iron-rich meals significantly improves absorption. Calcium and coffee within an hour of iron supplementation inhibit absorption.
iron_deficiency.action: Review your diet for regular iron sources. If you're supplementing without dietary changes, your levels may be harder to maintain long-term.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 24 — are you absorbing what you're taking?
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, Vitamin D is fat-soluble and absorbs best with food containing fat. Calcium supplements should be split into doses under 500mg for optimal absorption. Taking both at the same time can reduce absorption of each. These details matter for whether your supplementation is actually working.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Review how you're taking your supplements with your GP or pharmacist. Timing and dose splitting can make a significant difference to absorption.

track_prompt.prompt: You're entering an age range where some women start thinking more actively about family planning — whether that means understanding their health, thinking about timing, or just knowing what options exist. Want to explore what this looks like at your stage?

decisions.narrative: For the first time you feel genuinely competent. With that competence comes visibility — and a leadership opportunity has appeared.

choice_a.label: Step into leadership — take the responsibility and grow into it
choice_a.consequence: Managing people is completely different from being good at the work. You've learned that the hard way — and you're better for it.

choice_b.label: Stay as an individual contributor — go deeper, not wider
choice_b.consequence: Your specialist depth is becoming a genuine asset. People seek you out. You're building something distinctly yours.

decisions.narrative: Cervical screening is recommended from 25 in Singapore. You've been meaning to make health appointments more routine — this feels like the natural moment to actually do it.

choice_a.label: Book the cervical screening and make annual checkups a standing habit
choice_a.consequence: The screening was straightforward. Making it a habit was the important part. You know your baseline and you'll notice when it changes.

choice_b.label: I'll get to it when I have a gap
choice_b.consequence: Another year without a checkup. Nothing feels wrong — but you also can't know what your baseline looks like.

decisions.narrative: People around you are making increasingly different choices — some deepening commitments, some happily single, some navigating breakups. You're clearer on what you want than you were at 21.

choice_a.label: Be intentional about pursuing what I want — clarity is useful
choice_a.consequence: The intentionality paid off — not in getting exactly what you wanted, but in knowing what you were looking for when it appeared.

choice_b.label: Let things unfold — I trust the process
choice_b.consequence: Things unfolded. Whether in the direction you hoped or not, you stayed present to it.

default.headline: 25 — the year proactive health care starts paying off
default.body: Cervical screening from 25, a first gynaecology exam, thyroid and iron baseline, and a Vitamin D check together give you a reference map that makes every future appointment more meaningful. You are not looking for problems. You are establishing what your normal looks like so you can recognise when it changes.
default.action: Book a cervical screening appointment at your polyclinic. In Singapore it is recommended every 3 years from age 25 and takes 10 minutes.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 25 — metabolic monitoring is now the priority
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, by 25 the metabolic monitoring component of your management becomes as important as the hormonal component. Annual checks on fasting glucose, insulin, cholesterol, and blood pressure are the standard of care — these are the long-term risks that PCOS management is primarily trying to prevent.
pcos.action: Ask for a full metabolic panel at your next annual review: fasting glucose, insulin, lipid panel, and blood pressure alongside your hormonal markers.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 25 — stable management or time to reassess?
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, at 25 it is worth asking whether your condition has remained stable or requires a different management approach. If symptoms have worsened or lifestyle interventions haven't been sufficient, medication options are available. This is an appropriate age to have that conversation proactively.
pcod.action: Ask your GP at your next review: given my age and current symptom picture, is my management approach still optimal or are there better options now?

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 25 — specialist care should be established by now
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, if you are not yet under specialist care, 25 is the age to establish that relationship. A gynaecologist who knows your history, has performed or reviewed imaging, and has a long-term management plan is meaningfully different from reactive GP management of individual symptoms.
endometriosis.action: If you are still being managed at GP level, ask for a specialist referral this year. Bring your full symptom history including pain logs if you have them.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 25 — do you have a clear monitoring schedule?
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, a clear monitoring schedule — ultrasound every 12 to 18 months with a consistent clinician who can compare results over time — is the most important thing at 25. Without longitudinal comparison, individual scans tell you size but not trajectory.
fibroids.action: Confirm you have a monitoring schedule with a consistent clinician. Ask specifically: when is my next ultrasound, and who will compare it against my previous results?

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 25 — annual monitoring is the baseline
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, annual TSH monitoring from 25 onward is standard — not just when you feel symptoms. Thyroid function can drift gradually in ways that don't register as acute symptoms until the shift is significant. Annual testing catches drift early.
thyroid.action: Confirm your next annual thyroid review is scheduled. If it's been more than 12 months since your last test and you haven't booked one, do that now.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 25 — are heavy periods the underlying driver?
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, heavy menstrual bleeding is one of the most common ongoing causes. If your iron keeps dropping despite supplementation, the monthly loss may be outpacing what you can replace. This is a medical issue — not a lifestyle one — and treatment options for heavy bleeding are available.
iron_deficiency.action: If your iron levels are not maintaining despite supplementation, ask your GP specifically whether your menstrual blood loss is the driving factor and what treatment options are available.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 25 — the last major bone-building window
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, 25 to 30 is the final significant bone density building window. After your late twenties the bone-building process slows dramatically. Correcting deficiency now and maintaining adequate levels through this period is the most impactful thing you can do for your skeletal health over the next 50 years.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm your Vitamin D level has reached the target range — 75 nmol/L or above. If not, discuss whether your current supplement dose is sufficient.

decisions.narrative: You're entering a new tier. The work is more complex, the stakes higher, and the people around you more capable. How you position yourself now shapes the next five years.

choice_a.label: Become the specialist — deep expertise in one domain
choice_a.consequence: You've built something rare — genuine depth. People come to you specifically. It's starting to make you indispensable in a way generalism couldn't.

choice_b.label: Build breadth — understand the whole system
choice_b.consequence: Your ability to connect dots across domains is becoming your edge. It's harder to title but easier to leverage across contexts.

decisions.narrative: You've been managing painful periods for years. A colleague just told you she was finally diagnosed with endometriosis after 8 years of being dismissed. The average diagnosis delay is 7 to 10 years.

choice_a.label: Investigate properly — painful periods that disrupt your life are not normal
choice_a.consequence: The investigation took persistence. You have an answer now — and a management plan. The relief of knowing outweighs the difficulty of finding out.

choice_b.label: Manage the pain and carry on
choice_b.consequence: You're still managing. The pain is still there. The question of what's causing it is still unanswered.

decisions.narrative: A relationship is asking for a real commitment — time, geography, shared future plans. It's not a casual ask.

choice_a.label: Commit — this is worth it
choice_a.consequence: The commitment changed the relationship and changed you. You've learned what choosing someone deliberately actually means.

choice_b.label: Not at this level — not right now
choice_b.consequence: You honoured what you actually wanted rather than what felt expected. The relationship adjusted — or it ended. Both were honest outcomes.

default.headline: Endometriosis — if symptoms have been dismissed, push again now
default.body: Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 women with an average diagnosis delay of 7 to 10 years. If your painful periods started in your mid-to-late teens and have been dismissed as normal, you may still be undiagnosed. Period pain requiring regular painkillers or causing you to miss work or plans is a clinical symptom — not a personality trait.
default.action: Document pain levels across one cycle rated 1 to 10 each day. If you regularly rate above 6, bring that log to a GP and ask specifically for a gynaecology referral.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 26 — staying ahead of long-term risks
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, by 26 the long-term metabolic picture is increasingly relevant. Annual monitoring of fasting glucose, insulin, cholesterol, and blood pressure catches the conditions PCOS predisposes you to — Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease — at the earliest and most treatable stage.
pcos.action: Ensure your annual PCOS review includes a full metabolic panel, not just hormonal markers. These metabolic checks are the core of long-term PCOS management.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 26 — has anything changed?
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, 26 is a good age for a reassessment — particularly if your lifestyle has changed significantly since diagnosis. Stress levels, diet, exercise, and weight all affect PCOD. An updated ultrasound and hormonal comparison tells you whether you're managing effectively or whether the picture has shifted.
pcod.action: Book a PCOD reassessment including updated ultrasound and hormonal panel if it's been more than 18 months since your last full review.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 26 — quality of life review
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, at 26 a quality of life assessment is as important as a clinical one. If your pain, fatigue, or mental health burden from endometriosis is significantly affecting your daily life, your current management plan may not be adequate even if it's technically treating the condition.
endometriosis.action: Rate the impact of endometriosis on your quality of life out of 10. If it's consistently above 5, your management plan deserves a specialist review focused on your lived experience.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 26 — treatment options are available if symptoms warrant
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, by 26 the question shifts from just monitoring to whether symptoms warrant treatment. Heavy bleeding, pelvic pressure, or frequent urination that significantly affects your quality of life are valid clinical reasons for treatment — not just ongoing monitoring.
fibroids.action: At your next monitoring appointment, ask specifically: given the symptoms I have, is continued monitoring still the right approach or are treatment options now appropriate?

thyroid.headline: Thyroid and career stress at 26
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, career acceleration in your mid-twenties often coincides with sustained stress — which can affect thyroid function and may require dose adjustment. Hashimoto's thyroiditis in particular can worsen during stressful periods. If energy, weight, mood, or hair have shifted, don't wait for your scheduled check.
thyroid.action: If you've had a significantly stressful year and your symptoms have changed, request a thyroid check now. Include thyroid antibodies (TPO) if you haven't tested these recently.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 26 — is it being managed or just monitored?
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, there's a difference between monitoring levels and actively managing them. If your ferritin is consistently low despite supplementation, the approach needs to change — not just continue. IV iron infusion, treating the source of blood loss, or dietary optimisation are all options depending on the cause.
iron_deficiency.action: If your ferritin has not reached the target level of 30 micrograms per litre despite supplementation for 6 months or more, ask your GP to investigate why and discuss alternative approaches.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 26 — maintaining what you've built
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, by 26 the focus shifts from building bone density to maintaining it. Annual Vitamin D monitoring ensures your levels stay in the adequate range through this critical decade. Dropping back into deficiency after correction is common without ongoing monitoring.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Book an annual Vitamin D check as a standing appointment if you haven't already. Maintaining levels requires monitoring, not just supplementing.

decisions.narrative: You know what you're good at and you know what you want to build. The question at 27 is how to position yourself for the next decade — not just the next role.

choice_a.label: Build toward leadership — scale your impact through others
choice_a.consequence: You're building a team. It's harder than the individual work ever was — and more meaningful in ways you didn't expect.

choice_b.label: Build toward expertise and influence — become the person others come to
choice_b.consequence: Your reputation is becoming your career. People know what you stand for professionally. That clarity is its own currency.

decisions.narrative: A colleague mentions she just had her AMH tested — a blood test that gives an indication of ovarian reserve. Not as a fertility verdict, she says, but as a planning data point she wanted to have.

choice_a.label: Look into it — I want the data point
choice_a.consequence: Your AMH result gave you information you didn't have before. It changed how you think about your options over the next decade — not with urgency, but with clarity.

choice_b.label: Not relevant to me right now
choice_b.consequence: You've noted that it exists. You don't have that data point yet. That may or may not matter later.

decisions.narrative: Bigger life questions are arriving. Someone important in your life wants to know where things are going. So do you.

choice_a.label: Have the direct conversation about the future
choice_a.consequence: The conversation was one of the most important you've had. Whatever the outcome, you're no longer guessing.

choice_b.label: Give it more time — these things reveal themselves
choice_b.consequence: You waited. The relationship either found its own clarity or the question got bigger. Waiting was a choice too.

default.headline: AMH — a planning tool, not a verdict
default.body: AMH is a blood test that indicates ovarian reserve — the number of eggs remaining. It is not a fertility test and does not predict whether you will conceive. It is a data point. Knowing your AMH at 27 gives you a reference number that can inform decisions over the next decade regardless of whether children are part of your plan.
default.action: Ask your GP or a private clinic about an AMH test. It is a single blood test available privately in Singapore for approximately SGD 80 to 150.

pcos.headline: AMH and PCOS — your number means something different
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, your AMH level is likely elevated relative to standard reference ranges — not because of higher fertility but because of the higher number of small follicles characteristic of PCOS. A standard AMH interpretation does not apply to you directly. This is important to understand before you interpret any results.
pcos.action: If you get an AMH test, discuss results specifically with a gynaecologist who understands PCOS — not just a GP. The interpretation is different for your condition.

pcod.headline: AMH with PCOD — understanding your picture
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, like PCOS, your AMH may appear elevated due to follicle accumulation rather than reflecting true reserve. A specialist interpretation of your AMH alongside an antral follicle count gives a more accurate picture than the number alone.
pcod.action: Ask for an antral follicle count ultrasound alongside your AMH test for a more complete ovarian picture.

endometriosis.headline: AMH and endometriosis — why earlier testing matters for you
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, ovarian endometriomas can reduce ovarian reserve over time through inflammation and surgical intervention. This makes an AMH baseline more relevant for you than for women without the condition. Having a number now gives you a reference point as your condition evolves.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist about your ovarian reserve specifically as part of your next review — not just your symptom management.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 27 — impact on quality of life and planning
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, by 27 the impact on quality of life is the primary clinical consideration. If heavy bleeding, pain, or pressure symptoms are significantly affecting your life, treatment options that were premature at 22 may now be appropriate. You don't have to continue managing symptoms indefinitely.
fibroids.action: At your next review, ask specifically about treatment options if your symptoms are affecting your quality of life — surgical and non-surgical options are both available.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health and long-term planning at 27
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid hormones affect bone density and cardiovascular health over the long term — not just energy and weight. At 27 it is worth a broader conversation about how your thyroid management accounts for these longer-term health outcomes, not just TSH normalisation.
thyroid.action: Ask your doctor how your thyroid management accounts for long-term bone density and cardiovascular health, not just keeping TSH in the normal range.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 27 — the long-term management approach
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, by 27 it is worth establishing whether this is a permanent management situation — common for women with heavy periods — or something that can be resolved. If you've been supplementing on and off for years without sustained correction, the underlying cause may need addressing directly.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP: is my iron deficiency a condition to manage long-term or something that can be resolved? The answer changes the approach.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 27 — maintaining adequate levels
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, annual monitoring keeps your levels in the adequate range through this critical decade. Vitamin D in particular fluctuates with season, sun exposure, and lifestyle. Indoor-heavy work environments common in your late twenties can reduce levels even after correction.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Annual Vitamin D testing from 27 onward keeps you from unknowingly dropping back into deficiency. Book this as a standing annual appointment.

track_prompt.prompt: You're in an age range where understanding your reproductive health options — whatever you decide to do with them — is genuinely useful. Want to explore what family planning looks like at this stage, including what the timeline actually says?

decisions.narrative: A significant fork has appeared. Further education — an MBA, a specialist qualification — could accelerate things. So could staying and executing. Both are legitimate paths.

choice_a.label: Invest in further education — credential and network are worth it
choice_a.consequence: The program was intense and expensive and worth it. You came out with a different perspective on what's possible and a different network to work with.

choice_b.label: Stay and execute — experience compounds faster than credentials
choice_b.consequence: You built real track record while others were in classrooms. The gap between you and your peers is experience, not qualification.

decisions.narrative: A friend is going through the egg freezing process. It's more routine than you expected — she's working through the whole thing without taking leave.

choice_a.label: Learn what it actually involves — I want to make an informed decision
choice_a.consequence: You now understand what egg freezing is — the process, the costs, the realistic success rates. Whether you pursue it or not, you made an informed choice rather than an uninformed one.

choice_b.label: Not relevant to me right now
choice_b.consequence: You've noted it exists. The details remain vague. That may be fine — or it may be a gap you'll want to fill later.

decisions.narrative: Cohabitation, marriage, or a deliberate choice to build your life independently — the question of long-term life structure is becoming concrete rather than theoretical.

choice_a.label: Build a shared life — commit to a structure with someone
choice_a.consequence: Building a life with someone is different from being in a relationship with them. You're learning that distinction in real time.

choice_b.label: Build an independent life structure on your terms
choice_b.consequence: You've built something that's entirely yours. The freedom is real. So is the intentionality required to maintain it.

default.headline: Egg freezing — the honest picture
default.body: Egg freezing involves 2 to 3 weeks of hormone injections and monitoring appointments, followed by an egg retrieval procedure. In Singapore one cycle costs approximately SGD 10,000 to 15,000 including medication. Success rates per frozen egg are meaningfully better at 28 to 30 than at 35. It preserves options — it does not guarantee outcomes.
default.action: A consultation with a fertility clinic is free or low cost at most Singapore private clinics. You'll get personalised information based on your AMH and current health.

pcos.headline: Egg freezing with PCOS — what's different for you
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, you typically respond strongly to ovarian stimulation — often producing more eggs per cycle than average. This requires careful monitoring to avoid ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS). A clinic experienced in PCOS management is important. Ask specifically about their OHSS protocol.
pcos.action: When consulting fertility clinics, ask: what is your specific protocol for PCOS patients and how do you monitor for OHSS? The answer tells you about their competence.

pcod.headline: Egg freezing with PCOD
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, similar to PCOS, ovarian stimulation may produce a higher than average response. The risk of OHSS exists and requires active monitoring. Discussing your condition in detail with a fertility specialist before starting any stimulation cycle is important.
pcod.action: Disclose your PCOD diagnosis at any fertility consultation and ask how it affects their stimulation protocol specifically.

endometriosis.headline: Egg freezing with endometriosis — timing is more relevant for you
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, ovarian endometriomas can reduce your reserve over time. This makes the timing of egg freezing more relevant for you than for women without the condition — earlier is generally better. A specialist should assess your current reserve before advising on whether and when to proceed.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist: given my condition and current ovarian reserve, is there a window where egg freezing would be most beneficial for me?

fibroids.headline: Fibroids and egg freezing — what to know
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, their location and size can affect the egg freezing and future embryo transfer processes. Submucosal fibroids in particular can affect implantation. A fertility specialist will assess whether any fibroid treatment is advisable before proceeding with egg freezing.
fibroids.action: Include your fibroid diagnosis and most recent ultrasound findings in any fertility consultation so the specialist can assess whether they affect the process.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid function and egg freezing
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid hormones affect ovarian response to stimulation. Suboptimal thyroid function — even within broad normal range — can affect egg quality and quantity. Ensuring optimal thyroid management before a cycle is worth discussing with your clinic.
thyroid.action: Tell your fertility clinic about your thyroid condition and ask whether your current TSH level is optimal for stimulation or whether it should be reviewed first.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency and hormonal health at 28
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, iron plays a role in ovarian function and egg quality. Chronic iron deficiency can affect mitochondrial function in cells — including egg cells. Correcting iron deficiency before any assisted reproductive procedure is standard clinical advice.
iron_deficiency.action: If you're considering egg freezing or fertility investigation, ask your GP to confirm your ferritin is in the adequate range before proceeding.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Vitamin D and reproductive health at 28
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, Vitamin D receptors are present in ovarian tissue and emerging research suggests adequate Vitamin D levels support ovarian function. While the evidence is still developing, maintaining adequate Vitamin D is a low-cost, low-risk intervention with multiple health benefits.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm your Vitamin D level is in the adequate range — 75 nmol/L or above — before any fertility-related procedure.

track_prompt.prompt: You're in an age range where family planning decisions — whether that's trying to conceive, exploring fertility preservation, or making a definitive choice not to — are most commonly made. Want to explore what's available and what the timeline actually looks like?

decisions.narrative: You're thinking in five-year arcs now. The question isn't what your next role is — it's what you're building toward and whether your current path leads there.

choice_a.label: Map a clear trajectory — specific goals, specific timeline
choice_a.consequence: The clarity is motivating and pressurising in equal measure. You know where you're going — and you know when you're off track.

choice_b.label: Stay responsive — the best opportunities aren't always planned
choice_b.consequence: You stayed open and something unexpected appeared. Whether it's better than a plan would have been is a question you can't answer.

decisions.narrative: A mentor a decade ahead of you mentions that the habits she built at 29 shaped her forties in ways she didn't expect. She's talking about health as much as career.

choice_a.label: Take health seriously as a long-term asset — build the habits now
choice_a.consequence: The habits took six months to stick. By the end of the year they're infrastructure rather than effort.

choice_b.label: I'll build better habits when life is less intense
choice_b.consequence: Life remained intense. The habits are still pending. This is the third year you've said this.

decisions.narrative: The social landscape at 29 is diverging sharply — engagements, children, relocations, career pivots. You're clear on what you want your life to look like. Staying connected across different life choices is its own skill.

choice_a.label: Stay connected to people making different choices — breadth enriches
choice_a.consequence: The breadth of your relationships has given you a wider picture of what a good life can look like.

choice_b.label: Invest in people whose lives align with yours — depth over breadth
choice_b.consequence: Your inner circle is smaller and more aligned. The conversations are deeper. The support is more specific.

default.headline: The habits at 29 that shape your forties
default.body: Perimenopause begins on average between 45 and 51 — which is 16 to 22 years from now. But the lifestyle factors that shape how it unfolds are being set right now. Bone density, cardiovascular health, and metabolic function are all being shaped today by your sleep, movement, nutrition, and stress levels.
default.action: Identify one health habit that is currently inconsistent — sleep, exercise, or nutrition — and build it into your routine before 30. These compound in the same way career choices do.

pcos.headline: PCOS and the decade ahead
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, the metabolic risks — insulin resistance, cardiovascular disease, Type 2 diabetes — become more clinically relevant as you move through your thirties. The habits you build now around diet, movement, and metabolic monitoring are your most effective long-term intervention. Annual metabolic reviews are the clinical minimum.
pcos.action: Schedule an annual metabolic review as a recurring appointment — fasting glucose, insulin, lipid panel, blood pressure. This is your long-term PCOS management infrastructure.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 29 — long-term management strategy
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, by 29 a clear long-term management strategy is appropriate. Whether your condition has remained stable or evolved, having a plan that covers the next decade — including metabolic monitoring and symptom management — is more useful than reactive annual checkups.
pcod.action: Ask your GP or specialist: what does long-term management of my condition look like through my thirties? What should I be monitoring annually?

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis and long-term quality of life
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, at 29 the question is whether your condition is being actively managed for your quality of life or just monitored. Pain management strategies, hormonal treatments, and surgical options all have different profiles at this age. A specialist who treats quality of life, not just clinical markers, is worth finding.
endometriosis.action: Ask your specialist specifically: what is the best approach for my quality of life over the next 10 years, not just my current symptom burden?

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 29 — treatment decision point
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, by 29 the question of whether symptoms warrant treatment rather than ongoing monitoring becomes more pressing. Non-surgical options — UAE, medication — are available alongside surgical ones. The decision depends on symptom severity, fibroid characteristics, and your future plans.
fibroids.action: Ask your doctor for a comprehensive treatment options review — not just monitoring. Ask which options are appropriate for your specific fibroids and symptom picture.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health going into your thirties
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid management may require adjustment as your body changes through your late twenties. Stress, weight changes, and other hormonal shifts can all affect thyroid function. Annual monitoring with a consistent clinician who knows your history is the standard going into your thirties.
thyroid.action: Confirm annual thyroid monitoring is in your calendar. If you've had significant life changes — stress, weight change, other health issues — request a check now rather than waiting.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 29 — resolving it or managing it long-term
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, by 29 it is worth a definitive conversation about whether this is manageable through diet and supplementation or whether the underlying cause — typically heavy menstrual bleeding — needs direct treatment. Treating the source is more sustainable than indefinite supplementation.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP: given how long I've had iron deficiency and my current management approach, is this sustainable long-term or should we address the underlying cause directly?

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 29 — protecting what you've built
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, by 29 the bone density building phase is largely complete. The focus now is on maintaining what you've built. Annual Vitamin D monitoring and consistent calcium intake through diet and supplementation are the ongoing requirements.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Annual Vitamin D testing is your maintenance tool from here. Book it as a standing appointment alongside your other annual health checks.

decisions.narrative: 30 arrives with more internal noise than you expected. You're reassessing everything — not because something is wrong, but because something has shifted. What does the next decade actually look like?

choice_a.label: Recommit — you're building something real and you're not done
choice_a.consequence: You recommitted with clarity rather than inertia. The next decade has a shape now and you chose it.

choice_b.label: Redesign — 30 is early enough to build something completely different
choice_b.consequence: The redesign is disorienting and liberating in equal measure. You're starting something. That matters.

decisions.narrative: A comprehensive health review at 30 feels like the right thing to do — a full picture of where you are before the next decade.

choice_a.label: Book a comprehensive health review — I want to know my baseline at 30
choice_a.consequence: The full picture was more useful than individual data points ever were. You know your body at 30. That knowledge is yours now.

choice_b.label: I'll get to it — things are fine as far as I can tell
choice_b.consequence: You enter your thirties without a comprehensive baseline. Things may well be fine. You genuinely can't know.

decisions.narrative: The question of long-term life structure is no longer abstract. Whatever you're building — with a partner, independently, or something in between — this is the decade it becomes real.

choice_a.label: Build the life structure I want deliberately — make it concrete
choice_a.consequence: Making it concrete made it real. The life you're building looks like a choice now, not a default.

choice_b.label: Let it unfold — I trust where I'm going
choice_b.consequence: You're living the life you have rather than the life you planned. There's something right about that.

default.headline: Your full health baseline at 30
default.body: A comprehensive review at 30 covers your hormonal panel, AMH if not tested recently, thyroid, iron, Vitamin D, pelvic ultrasound, cervical screening if due, blood pressure, and fasting glucose. This is not a single appointment — it's a set of tests that together give you a complete picture. At 30 you have enough health history to make these results genuinely meaningful.
default.action: Ask your GP to help you coordinate a 30-year health baseline review covering: full hormonal panel, thyroid, iron, Vitamin D, fasting glucose, blood pressure, and pelvic ultrasound.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 30 — the decade that matters most for management
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, your thirties are when the long-term metabolic risks become most clinically relevant. Sustained management of insulin resistance, weight, and hormonal balance in your thirties significantly affects your cardiovascular health, diabetes risk, and quality of life in your forties and fifties.
pcos.action: Ask your GP or endocrinologist for a forward-looking PCOS management strategy for your thirties — not just your current symptoms.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 30 — decade review
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, at 30 a decade review of your condition — where it started, where it is now, and what the next 10 years of management look like — is appropriate. Whether your condition has remained stable or evolved, having a clear plan is more useful than annual reactive monitoring.
pcod.action: Request a comprehensive PCOD review at 30 that looks backward at your history and forward at your management strategy.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 30 — taking comprehensive stock
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, at 30 a full review covers current symptom burden, quality of life, ovarian reserve, and whether your current treatment is still the right one. Endometriosis changes over time and management appropriate at 25 may not be optimal at 30.
endometriosis.action: Request a comprehensive endometriosis review — symptom burden, quality of life, ovarian reserve, and forward-looking management plan for your thirties.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 30 — planning the decade ahead
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, by 30 a clear plan for the next decade is appropriate — whether that's continued monitoring, treatment for symptomatic fibroids, or a decision about timing if pregnancy is part of your plans. Fibroids tend to peak in size in the thirties before shrinking after menopause.
fibroids.action: Ask for a comprehensive fibroid management plan that covers the next 10 years — not just your next monitoring appointment.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 30 — the full picture
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, at 30 thyroid management should be reviewed holistically — not just TSH normalisation but the impact on bone density, cardiovascular health, and mental health. If you've been managed to a range rather than to optimal function, this is worth revisiting.
thyroid.action: Ask for a thyroid review that includes Free T3, Free T4, TSH, and thyroid antibodies. Ask how your thyroid management connects to your long-term bone density and cardiovascular health.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 30 — resolved or ongoing?
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, by 30 you should have a clear picture of whether this is a resolved condition, a managed chronic one, or a symptom of something that needs direct treatment. If you're still supplementing reactively without a stable strategy, a definitive conversation with your GP is overdue.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP: is my iron deficiency resolved, managed, or unresolved? What does long-term management look like for my specific situation?

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 30 — long-term maintenance
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, by 30 the management goal is consistent maintenance of adequate levels for the rest of your life. This is a lifestyle management situation with annual monitoring — not a short-term correction. Building it into your annual health review is the most sustainable approach.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Build annual Vitamin D testing into your standard health review from 30 onward. Consistent maintenance from here protects bone density through perimenopause and beyond.

track_prompt.prompt: At 30, some women want to think seriously about family planning — the window where success rates for both natural conception and egg freezing are still strong. Others want to think about long-term health without family planning in the picture. Which feels more relevant to you right now?
