Tell me about long-term health without family planning

decisions.narrative: You're in the middle of building something real. The question is no longer whether you can do this — it's whether this is what you want to keep doing.

choice_a.label: Double down — this is your decade and you're not done
choice_a.consequence: The recommitment brought clarity. You're not drifting. You're building.

choice_b.label: Start exploring what else is possible — while you still have the energy
choice_b.consequence: The exploration unsettled things productively. You're not sure yet where it leads but you're more honest about what you want.

decisions.narrative: Your annual health review reveals something that's been creeping up — not dramatic, but worth addressing. Small signals matter more at 31 than they did at 23.

choice_a.label: Address it now — small things caught early are manageable
choice_a.consequence: Catching it early made the intervention simple. You're glad you paid attention.

choice_b.label: Monitor it — I don't want to overreact
choice_b.consequence: You're monitoring. The thing you noticed is still there. The line between prudent watching and avoidance is worth examining.

decisions.narrative: The life structure you've built — with someone or independently — is being stress-tested by the demands of your thirties. How you navigate this shapes the decade.

choice_a.label: Invest in the relationship infrastructure — it requires active maintenance
choice_a.consequence: The investment paid off. The relationship is a source of support rather than another demand.

choice_b.label: Trust that the foundation is strong enough to hold
choice_b.consequence: The foundation held — or it revealed it was less solid than you thought. Either way you know now.

default.headline: Annual health review at 31 — what to include
default.body: From your early thirties, annual health reviews should consistently include: cervical screening if due, full blood count including ferritin, thyroid function, fasting glucose and blood pressure, and a pelvic ultrasound every 2 to 3 years or sooner if symptomatic. These together give you a longitudinal picture — each result more meaningful than the last.
default.action: Ensure your annual review covers: blood pressure, fasting glucose, thyroid, ferritin, and cervical screening schedule. These are your core monitoring markers from 30 onward.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 31 — is your management keeping pace with your life?
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, lifestyle changes in your early thirties — changes in diet, activity, stress, or weight — affect your metabolic markers. Management that was optimal at 28 may need adjustment now. Annual reviews that compare your current metabolic picture against your baseline catch drift early.
pcos.action: Bring your previous metabolic results to your next annual review and ask for a year-on-year comparison. Trends matter more than individual readings.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 31 — stability or change?
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, an ultrasound comparison against your baseline at this age tells you whether your condition is stable or has evolved. PCOD in the thirties often stabilises with established lifestyle management — confirming that stability is reassuring and clinically useful.
pcod.action: Request an ultrasound comparison against your earliest imaging. Confirmed stability is a useful clinical data point.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 31 — managing the full impact
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, the physical, mental health, and career impact of the condition should all be part of your management conversation at 31. If any dimension is not well-managed — pain, fatigue, mental health, or the practical impact on your work — that dimension deserves specific attention.
endometriosis.action: Rate each dimension of your endometriosis impact out of 10: pain, fatigue, mental health, work impact. Bring those ratings to your next specialist appointment.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 31 — growth monitoring and planning
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, your thirties are when fibroid growth is typically most active. Regular monitoring — ultrasound every 12 to 18 months — gives you a growth trajectory. If growth is rapid or symptoms are worsening, treatment before fibroids become very large is generally simpler.
fibroids.action: Ensure you have an ultrasound scheduled within the next 12 months. Ask your doctor about your fibroid's growth rate based on your imaging history.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid at 31 — annual monitoring is non-negotiable
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, annual TSH monitoring from your early thirties is the consistent standard. If you've missed an annual check, book one now. Thyroid function can shift in ways that don't register acutely until the drift is significant.
thyroid.action: If it's been more than 12 months since your last thyroid check, book one now rather than waiting for your next scheduled appointment.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 31 — the chronic management approach
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, by 31 it is likely a long-term management situation rather than a short-term correction. Establishing a sustainable approach — dietary, supplementation, and treating the source if heavy bleeding is the driver — is more useful than continuing to react to low levels.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP for a long-term iron management plan appropriate for your thirties — one that doesn't require you to react to results each year.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 31 — consistent maintenance
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, consistent maintenance of adequate levels through your thirties protects bone density through the hormonal changes of perimenopause. Annual testing is your early warning system.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm your annual Vitamin D test is scheduled. If levels have dropped below the adequate range, discuss whether your supplement dose needs adjusting.

decisions.narrative: You've arrived at a level of capability and reputation that opens doors. The question is which doors to walk through — and which to close intentionally.

choice_a.label: Focus — close some doors to go deeper through the right ones
choice_a.consequence: The focus has accelerated things in a way that trying to do everything never could. You know what you're for.

choice_b.label: Keep multiple options open — optionality has value
choice_b.consequence: You've maintained flexibility. Some opportunities required it. Some were just distraction. Telling the difference is the skill you're developing.

decisions.narrative: Hormonal changes in your early thirties are subtle but real. Some women start noticing shifts — in energy, in cycle patterns, in skin — that don't have an obvious explanation.

choice_a.label: Investigate changes proactively — I'd rather know
choice_a.consequence: Investigating gave you answers — some expected, some not. The ones you didn't expect were the most useful.

choice_b.label: These are probably just part of getting older
choice_b.consequence: You've normalised the changes. Some of them probably are normal. Some of them might be worth knowing about.

decisions.narrative: The relationships that have lasted to 32 have done so because they've adapted. The ones that haven't have taught you something. Either way you know yourself better than you did at 25.

choice_a.label: Invest in deepening the relationships that matter most
choice_a.consequence: The depth you've built is a genuine asset — to your wellbeing and to the people you've invested in.

choice_b.label: Some relationships have run their course — let them go with grace
choice_b.consequence: Releasing what's run its course made room for what's more aligned with who you are now.

default.headline: Hormonal shifts in your early thirties — what's normal and what's not
default.body: Subtle hormonal shifts begin in the early thirties — changes in cycle length, PMS symptoms, or energy that don't have an obvious lifestyle explanation. These are often perimenopause precursors that begin a decade or more before menopause. Baseline hormonal testing at 32 gives you a reference point for the changes ahead.
default.action: If you've noticed cycle changes, new PMS symptoms, or energy shifts without a lifestyle explanation, ask for a hormonal panel including FSH, LH, estradiol, and AMH.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 32 — metabolic risk management in your thirties
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, your thirties are the decade where proactive metabolic management has the highest return. Insulin resistance that is well-managed now significantly reduces your Type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk in your forties and fifties. This is the decade where it matters most.
pcos.action: Ask your endocrinologist for a metabolic risk assessment at 32 — not just monitoring, but an assessment of your 10-year cardiovascular and diabetes risk and what to do about it.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 32 — long-term stability assessment
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, by 32 most women with PCOD have a relatively stable picture if they've maintained lifestyle management. Confirming stability at this age — through imaging and hormonal panel — is reassuring and provides useful longitudinal data for your ongoing care.
pcod.action: Request a stability assessment at 32 — imaging and hormonal comparison against your earliest baseline — to confirm your condition is well-managed.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 32 — surgical options review
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, by 32 it may be appropriate to review whether any surgical intervention is warranted — either for symptom management, ovarian cyst treatment, or fertility-related reasons. The timing of surgery relative to other life plans matters and is worth a detailed specialist conversation.
endometriosis.action: Ask your specialist specifically: given my current symptom picture and life plans, is there a surgical option worth considering now or in the near future?

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 32 — active management decision
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, your early thirties are when fibroids are often most symptomatic. If you've been monitoring and symptoms are significantly affecting your quality of life, the calculus between ongoing monitoring and active treatment has likely shifted toward treatment being appropriate.
fibroids.action: Request a treatment options review rather than another monitoring appointment if your symptoms are significantly affecting your quality of life.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 32 — connected to the decade ahead
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid function affects bone density and cardiovascular health in measurable ways through your thirties. Optimal management — not just within-range management — protects these systems through the hormonal changes of your forties.
thyroid.action: Ask your doctor to review whether your current thyroid management is optimal for long-term bone and cardiovascular health, not just TSH normalisation.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 32 — addressing the source
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, if heavy menstrual bleeding is the underlying cause and you haven't addressed it directly, your thirties are the right time. Treatment options for heavy bleeding — hormonal, surgical, non-surgical — are well-established and can significantly improve your quality of life and iron status simultaneously.
iron_deficiency.action: If heavy periods are your iron deficiency driver and you haven't treated the bleeding directly, ask your GP for a treatment options review.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 32 — protecting bone density through your thirties
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, maintaining adequate levels through your thirties protects bone density against the accelerated loss that occurs in perimenopause. The habits established now are your buffer against osteoporosis.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Annual Vitamin D testing and consistent calcium intake are your maintenance requirements from 32 onward. Confirm both are in place at your annual review.

track_prompt.prompt: At 32, family planning decisions have a specific urgency for women who want children — natural conception rates shift meaningfully through this decade. Want honest information about what the options look like at this stage?

decisions.narrative: Your career has its own momentum now. The question is whether that momentum is taking you where you want to go — or just carrying you forward.

choice_a.label: Steer actively — the momentum is yours to direct
choice_a.consequence: Active steering felt uncomfortable at first — like disrupting something that was working. Then it became the most important thing you'd done in years.

choice_b.label: Trust the momentum — you've built well
choice_b.consequence: The momentum carried you. Whether to where you wanted to go is something only time will fully answer.

decisions.narrative: A comprehensive health picture at 33 looks different from the one at 25. You have history now — patterns visible across years rather than months.

choice_a.label: Review the longitudinal picture — what do the patterns show?
choice_a.consequence: The patterns were more informative than individual data points ever were. You know your body's tendencies now.

choice_b.label: If nothing acute is happening, I don't need to look too closely
choice_b.consequence: Nothing acute is happening. The patterns are still there, unexamined.

decisions.narrative: The relationships you've built by 33 are largely the ones you'll have for the long term. Tending them intentionally is different from the reactive relationship management of your twenties.

choice_a.label: Tend them actively — good relationships require maintenance
choice_a.consequence: The intentional tending paid off. The relationships are a genuine source of strength.

choice_b.label: The good ones sustain themselves
choice_b.consequence: Some do. Some needed more than you gave them. You've learned to tell the difference.

default.headline: Longitudinal health patterns at 33 — what years of data show
default.body: By 33 you have enough health history to see patterns that single appointments can't. Trends in ferritin, thyroid, blood pressure, weight, and cycle patterns over years are more clinically meaningful than any individual result. A clinician who reviews your longitudinal picture — not just your most recent numbers — is giving you genuinely better care.
default.action: Ask your GP to pull your results from the last 5 years and review them longitudinally. Ask: what patterns do you see, and what do they suggest about my health trajectory?

pcos.headline: PCOS at 33 — a decade of management, what have you learned?
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, by 33 you have enough management history to assess what has and hasn't worked. A reflective review with your specialist — what interventions have been most effective, what your metabolic trajectory looks like, what the next decade of management should prioritise — is valuable at this stage.
pcos.action: Ask your specialist for a reflective management review: what has worked, what hasn't, and what should the focus be for the next 10 years?

pcod.headline: PCOD at 33 — stable management confirmation
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, confirming stable management at 33 — through comparison imaging and hormonal panel — provides reassurance and useful longitudinal data. If symptoms have changed, this is also the time to reassess the management approach.
pcod.action: Request a longitudinal comparison at your next review — current imaging and hormonal results against your earliest baseline.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 33 — quality of life over the long term
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, by 33 the cumulative impact on quality of life — pain burden, mental health, career impact — is worth assessing comprehensively. If any dimension is not well-managed, specific interventions exist. Accepting ongoing significant quality of life impact is not the standard of care.
endometriosis.action: Complete a comprehensive quality of life assessment for your endometriosis and bring it to your specialist. If any dimension is significantly impacted, ask what specific intervention addresses it.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 33 — the treatment window
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, your early thirties are often the most appropriate treatment window if symptoms warrant it. Treatment before fibroids become very large is generally simpler with faster recovery. If you've been monitoring while symptoms worsen, this is the year to have the definitive treatment conversation.
fibroids.action: If your symptoms have worsened over the last 2 years, ask for a definitive treatment options review rather than continued monitoring.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid at 33 — connected to perimenopause ahead
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid management becomes more complex through perimenopause — typically beginning in the mid-to-late forties. Establishing optimal management in your thirties, before those hormonal changes begin, is the most effective preparation.
thyroid.action: Ask your doctor specifically: how should my thyroid management evolve as I approach perimenopause, and what should I be monitoring more closely from my mid-thirties?

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 33 — sustainable management into perimenopause
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, menstrual blood loss typically increases in the perimenopause transition. Establishing sustainable iron management now — before that transition — means you're not starting from a deficit when blood loss potentially increases.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP: how do we ensure my iron management is sustainable through the hormonal changes of my late thirties and early forties?

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 33 — the perimenopause preparation window
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, adequate Vitamin D and calcium levels in your thirties meaningfully buffer against the accelerated bone loss that accompanies declining estrogen in perimenopause. This decade of maintenance has a payoff in your fifties and sixties.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm your annual Vitamin D test and calcium intake are consistent. Ask your GP how your current approach prepares you for the bone density changes of perimenopause.

decisions.narrative: At 34 your professional identity is largely established — but that doesn't mean it's fixed. The question is what you want the next chapter to look like, and whether you have the courage to ask for it.

choice_a.label: Ask for what I want — negotiate the career I actually want
choice_a.consequence: Asking for it was harder than you expected. So was the yes. Both were worth it.

choice_b.label: Build toward it — demonstrate the value first
choice_b.consequence: You're building the case. It's taking longer than asking would have. The case is getting stronger.

decisions.narrative: Preventive care at 34 is less about catching things early and more about maintaining the systems you've built. The habits are largely set. The question is whether they're the right ones.

choice_a.label: Review and optimise — are my health habits still the right ones at 34?
choice_a.consequence: The review revealed two things that needed updating. Small adjustments. Meaningful difference.

choice_b.label: If it's working, don't change it
choice_b.consequence: Consistency has value. The habits are holding. Whether they're optimal is a question you haven't asked.

decisions.narrative: The life you've built by 34 reflects your choices — including your relationship choices. You know what you value and what you won't compromise on.

choice_a.label: Keep investing in the relationships that reflect my values
choice_a.consequence: The investments compound. The relationships at 34 are among the most meaningful things in your life.

choice_b.label: Some things don't need tending — they just are
choice_b.consequence: The ones that just are — they're still there. The others have quietly receded.

default.headline: Preventive care at 34 — maintaining the systems
default.body: At 34 the focus of health care shifts from building to maintaining. Annual reviews, consistent monitoring of the markers you've been tracking, and staying ahead of the hormonal shifts that begin in the mid-to-late thirties are the priorities. The women who navigate perimenopause most effectively are the ones who weren't surprised by it.
default.action: Confirm all your annual health monitoring markers are in your calendar and that your GP has a longitudinal picture of your health — not just your most recent results.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 34 — preparing for perimenopause transition
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, approaching the perimenopause transition with PCOS requires specific preparation. Hormonal changes in perimenopause interact with PCOS in complex ways — some symptoms improve, some worsen. Discussing this with your endocrinologist before the transition begins is more useful than responding to it afterward.
pcos.action: Ask your endocrinologist specifically: how will perimenopause interact with my PCOS, and what should I be monitoring and adjusting from my mid-thirties?

pcod.headline: PCOD at 34 — approaching the transition
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, hormonal changes in perimenopause may affect your condition differently than they affect women without it. Understanding what to expect and what to monitor is valuable preparation for a transition that typically begins in the mid-to-late forties.
pcod.action: Ask your GP or specialist: how should I expect PCOD to interact with perimenopause, and what monitoring should I add from my mid-thirties?

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 34 — perimenopause and symptom changes
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, perimenopause typically brings symptom changes — sometimes improvement as estrogen declines, sometimes new patterns. Understanding what to expect allows you to distinguish expected changes from new developments worth investigating.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist: what symptom changes should I expect as I approach perimenopause, and what would warrant urgent attention versus watchful waiting?

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 34 — the treatment window may be closing
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, treatment before 35 is generally simpler with better recovery. If you've been monitoring symptomatic fibroids without treatment, 34 to 35 is the year to make a definitive decision rather than continue deferring.
fibroids.action: If you have symptomatic fibroids and haven't made a treatment decision, book a definitive treatment consultation this year. Deferring past 35 doesn't make the decision easier.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid at 34 — pre-perimenopause optimisation
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, optimising your thyroid management before perimenopause begins is significantly easier than adjusting during it. Perimenopause brings its own symptom complexity — fatigue, mood changes, weight shifts — that overlap with thyroid symptoms. A clear thyroid baseline in your mid-thirties makes that future picture more readable.
thyroid.action: Ask your doctor to establish an optimal thyroid management baseline before your mid-thirties — one that will give you a clear reference point when perimenopause symptoms begin.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 34 — the perimenopause transition ahead
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, the perimenopause transition can bring changes in menstrual pattern — sometimes heavier periods before they eventually cease. Sustainable iron management going into your late thirties means you're not starting the transition with depleted reserves.
iron_deficiency.action: Confirm your iron management approach is sustainable long-term and discuss with your GP what adjustments may be needed as your menstrual pattern changes in your late thirties and forties.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 34 — preparing for accelerated bone loss
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, the accelerated bone loss that accompanies declining estrogen in perimenopause makes adequate calcium and Vitamin D in your thirties directly protective. The buffer you build now reduces the magnitude of that loss.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm adequate Vitamin D and calcium levels at your annual review. Ask your GP: am I in the optimal range for protecting bone density through the perimenopause transition?

track_prompt.prompt: For women who want children, 34 to 35 is a significant decision point. Want honest, non-alarming information about what the options look like at this stage — including assisted reproduction?

decisions.narrative: Lea is fully grown now — steady beside you. You're 35. You've built something real. The question isn't what comes next. It's what you want the rest of your working life to be for.

choice_a.label: Scale what I've built — take it further than I imagined at 22
choice_a.consequence: You're taking it further. The 22-year-old version of you would be surprised. The 35-year-old version of you finds that unremarkable — which is its own kind of achievement.

choice_b.label: Reorient — I want the second half of my career to mean something different
choice_b.consequence: The reorientation is underway. It's slower than a fresh start and more considered. That's appropriate.

decisions.narrative: A comprehensive health review at 35 is both a milestone and a reference point. Everything you've learned about your body in the last 14 years comes together here.

choice_a.label: Make it comprehensive — this is the baseline for the next chapter
choice_a.consequence: The comprehensive picture was the most useful health information you'd ever had. You entered the next chapter knowing where you stood.

choice_b.label: Annual checkup is enough — I don't need a full audit
choice_b.consequence: You've maintained the annual habit. The full picture remains partial. For now that's your choice.

decisions.narrative: At 35, the relationships you've chosen — and the ones you've let go — reflect who you are. The question now is how to tend what matters most.

choice_a.label: Invest deeply in the few relationships that are genuinely central
choice_a.consequence: Deep investment in a few is a different strategy from broad investment in many. It's the right one for where you are.

choice_b.label: Keep the breadth — I value the diversity of my relationships
choice_b.consequence: The breadth is a genuine asset. Different people bring different things. You've learned to distinguish the nourishing from the depleting.

default.headline: 35 — the baseline for the next chapter
default.body: A comprehensive health review at 35 covers your full hormonal panel, AMH, thyroid, iron, Vitamin D, fasting glucose, blood pressure, pelvic ultrasound, cervical screening if due, and a bone density discussion. This is the reference point for everything that follows. At 35 you have enough health history for these results to be genuinely interpretable.
default.action: Book a comprehensive 35-year health review. Ask your GP to look at your longitudinal data — the last 5 to 10 years of results — alongside your current ones. Trends matter as much as numbers.

pcos.headline: PCOS at 35 — the decade of highest metabolic relevance
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, 35 to 45 is the decade when proactive metabolic management has the highest long-term impact. Women with PCOS who maintain good metabolic control through this decade have significantly better cardiovascular and metabolic outcomes in their fifties and sixties.
pcos.action: Ask your endocrinologist for a 35-year PCOS review that includes your 10-year metabolic risk assessment and a clear management strategy for the decade ahead.

pcod.headline: PCOD at 35 — long-term stability and the decade ahead
pcod.body: Since you have PCOD, at 35 confirming long-term stability and planning for the hormonal changes of the late thirties and forties is the appropriate focus. A comprehensive review at this milestone gives you a clear picture going into the second half of your reproductive years.
pcod.action: Request a comprehensive PCOD review at 35 — longitudinal comparison and forward-looking management plan for your late thirties and forties.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis at 35 — managing through the perimenopause transition
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, 35 is an appropriate age for a forward-looking specialist review that addresses what the perimenopause transition means for your specific condition. For many women, endometriosis symptoms improve as estrogen declines — but the transition itself can be complex.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist for a forward-looking review that specifically addresses the perimenopause transition and what to expect for your condition.

fibroids.headline: Fibroids at 35 — the natural trajectory
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, the natural trajectory from 35 onward is toward stabilisation and eventual shrinkage after menopause as estrogen declines. If you've been managing symptomatic fibroids through your thirties, the approach may appropriately shift toward managing symptoms through perimenopause rather than intervention.
fibroids.action: Ask your gynaecologist: given my fibroids' history and current status, what is the management approach through perimenopause and what should I watch for?

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health at 35 — the foundation for the decade ahead
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, optimal thyroid management at 35 is the foundation for managing perimenopause well. The symptom overlap between suboptimal thyroid function and perimenopause — fatigue, weight, mood, sleep — makes a clear thyroid baseline at this age essential for the decade ahead.
thyroid.action: Establish a comprehensive thyroid baseline at 35 — Free T3, Free T4, TSH, and antibodies — that will serve as your reference point through the perimenopause transition.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency at 35 — sustainable management through the transition
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, iron needs typically stabilise and eventually decrease as menstrual loss reduces through perimenopause and menopause. Establishing a sustainable management approach at 35 means you're well-positioned for that transition.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP: what does iron management look like through perimenopause for someone with my history? How do we plan for the changing menstrual pattern ahead?

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D at 35 — the most critical maintenance decade
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, 35 to 50 is the most critical decade for maintaining bone density. The accelerated bone loss that accompanies declining estrogen in perimenopause makes consistent calcium and Vitamin D maintenance from 35 onward the most impactful bone health intervention available.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Confirm adequate Vitamin D and calcium levels at your 35-year review. Ask your GP: is my current approach adequate to protect bone density through perimenopause?

track_prompt.prompt: At 35, women who want children often want honest information about their options — natural conception, IVF, egg donation, adoption are all real paths. Want a clear, non-alarmist picture of what's available?

track_family_planning_21_23.title: Family Planning — Early Awareness
track_family_planning_21_23.framing: At 21 to 23, family planning is about awareness and options — not urgency. This is the lowest-pressure window to understand what exists.

career_moment.narrative: Family planning at 21 is less about decisions and more about knowing what's out there. The women who navigate this best later in life are usually the ones who understood their options early — without pressure.

choice_a.label: Tell me what reproductive health options exist at my age
choice_a.consequence: You have a map now. Not a plan — a map. That's exactly what this stage requires.

choice_b.label: Tell me what I should know about my fertility health at 21
choice_b.consequence: You understand what your body is doing and what to pay attention to. That's genuinely useful regardless of what you decide later.

default.headline: What family planning actually means at 21
default.body: Family planning at 21 means understanding your options — contraception, fertility awareness, the things worth monitoring for your long-term health. It doesn't mean making decisions about children. Most women in their early twenties are in their most fertile years and have significant time for any path they choose.
default.action: The most useful thing at 21 is establishing your health baseline — knowing your cycle, understanding your body, and having a GP you trust. That foundation serves every path.

pcos.headline: Family planning with PCOS at 21 — what to know early
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, understanding how it affects fertility is useful information even at 21 — not because decisions are urgent, but because knowledge reduces anxiety later. PCOS is a very treatable cause of ovulation-related fertility challenges. Most women with PCOS who want children conceive, often with some clinical support.
pcos.action: Ask your GP or gynaecologist to explain what trying to conceive with PCOS would look like — not as an immediate plan, but as useful information for your future self.

endometriosis.headline: Family planning with endometriosis at 21
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, understanding the fertility implications of your condition early — before they become urgent — is one of the most useful things you can do. Not all women with endometriosis have fertility challenges, but knowing your specific picture early gives you options.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist about your current fertility picture — ovarian reserve, tube health — as informational context, not as a decision you need to make now.

track_family_planning_24_27.title: Family Planning — Active Consideration
track_family_planning_24_27.framing: At 24 to 27, family planning becomes an active consideration for many women — understanding timelines, pre-conception health, and how planning fits with career.

career_moment.narrative: You're in a window where family planning — whatever direction it goes — benefits from having real information. Not the fear-based version. The actual clinical picture.

choice_a.label: Tell me what the actual fertility timeline looks like at my age
choice_a.consequence: The actual data is more reassuring than the narrative suggests. You have time — and you know what affects that time.

choice_b.label: Tell me what pre-conception health looks like if I want children someday
choice_b.consequence: Pre-conception health is simpler than you expected. Folic acid, a baseline checkup, knowing your cycle. The groundwork is manageable.

default.headline: The fertility timeline at 24 to 27 — what the data actually says
default.body: At 24 to 27 you are in a strong biological window. Most women who want children and begin trying before 32 conceive within 12 months. The narrative of urgency at this age is not supported by clinical evidence. What does matter: knowing your AMH as a baseline, understanding your cycle, and managing any conditions that affect ovulation.
default.action: If you want a baseline data point, an AMH test is available privately in Singapore for SGD 80 to 150. It's informational — not predictive of whether you'll conceive.

pcos.headline: Fertility with PCOS at 24 to 27
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, the good news is that PCOS-related ovulation challenges are among the most treatable fertility presentations. Ovulation induction — typically first with Letrozole — has a high success rate. Most women with PCOS who want children do not require IVF.
pcos.action: Ask your GP or gynaecologist about ovulation induction options — not as something you need now, but so you know the pathway when you do.

endometriosis.headline: Fertility with endometriosis at 24 to 27
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, the fertility implications depend significantly on severity and location. Many women with endometriosis conceive naturally. Others benefit from early specialist involvement. The most useful thing at this stage is knowing your specific picture — ovarian reserve, tube health — from a specialist.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist specifically about your fertility picture at your next review. The answer is more nuanced than the general statistics suggest.

career_moment.narrative: Career and family planning are not in opposition — they're in conversation. The women who navigate this most successfully are the ones who understand both sides of the conversation.

choice_a.label: Tell me how pregnancy fits practically with a career at this stage
choice_a.consequence: The practical picture is more plannable than the silence around it suggests. Maternity leave, career timing, employer conversations — these are all navigable.

choice_b.label: Tell me about fertility preservation options if I want to delay
choice_b.consequence: Egg freezing at 24 to 27 has the best success rates of any window. You understand what's involved now — and whether it fits your situation.

default.headline: Singapore maternity leave and career timing
default.body: Government-paid maternity leave in Singapore is 16 weeks for Singapore citizen children. Many companies offer additional weeks. Shared Parental Leave allows partners to share some of that leave. Timing a pregnancy around a promotion cycle or major project is something women plan deliberately and successfully — it is plannable.
default.action: Find your company's maternity leave policy in your employment contract or HR handbook. Knowing your entitlements before you need them is part of planning.

pcos.headline: Fertility preservation with PCOS at 24 to 27
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, egg freezing is an option — though your response to stimulation may be stronger than average, requiring careful monitoring for OHSS. Your elevated AMH does not mean egg freezing is more or less beneficial than for women without PCOS — it means the process requires PCOS-specific expertise.
pcos.action: If considering egg freezing, consult a clinic experienced in PCOS management specifically. Ask about their OHSS monitoring protocol.

endometriosis.headline: Fertility preservation with endometriosis at 24 to 27
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, egg freezing in your mid-twenties may be particularly valuable if your condition carries a risk of ovarian reserve reduction over time. A specialist assessment of your ovarian reserve and the trajectory of your condition informs whether and when fertility preservation is advisable.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist: given my specific condition and ovarian reserve, is fertility preservation something I should consider, and when would be optimal?

track_family_planning_28_31.title: Family Planning — Decision Window
track_family_planning_28_31.framing: At 28 to 31, family planning decisions — trying to conceive, fertility preservation, or making a definitive choice not to — are most commonly made. Real information supports real decisions.

career_moment.narrative: This is the window where the decision — whatever it is — benefits from being made with real information rather than assumptions, anxiety, or avoidance.

choice_a.label: I want to understand what trying to conceive looks like at this age
choice_a.consequence: The picture is clearer than you expected. The timeline is realistic. The options, if natural conception needs support, are well-established.

choice_b.label: I want to understand egg freezing in detail — process, costs, realistic outcomes
choice_b.consequence: You understand what egg freezing actually involves. The process is more routine than the language around it suggests. The costs are real. The success rates are honest.

default.headline: Trying to conceive at 28 to 31 — the realistic picture
default.body: Natural conception rates at 28 to 31 remain strong — roughly 80 to 85 percent of women who try for 12 months will conceive. The decline in fertility is gradual through the twenties and becomes more notable from the mid-thirties. Most women in this window who want children and try have a good statistical chance of natural conception.
default.action: A pre-conception checkup 3 to 6 months before trying covers: folic acid, rubella immunity, cycle health, any condition management, and baseline bloodwork. Book this before you need it.

pcos.headline: Trying to conceive with PCOS at 28 to 31
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, the most common first-line intervention for ovulation-related fertility challenges is Letrozole — an oral medication with a high success rate. Most women with PCOS who want to conceive at this age do so within 2 to 3 treatment cycles. IVF is a later step, not the first one.
pcos.action: Book a pre-conception consultation with a gynaecologist who specialises in PCOS. Ask to discuss the ovulation induction pathway before you start trying, so you know what the support options are.

endometriosis.headline: Trying to conceive with endometriosis at 28 to 31
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, your fertility picture depends on your specific diagnosis — severity, location, and ovarian reserve. Many women with endometriosis conceive naturally. Others benefit from early specialist involvement or assisted reproduction. A personalised assessment from your specialist is more useful than general statistics.
endometriosis.action: Ask your endometriosis specialist for a pre-conception fertility assessment — your current ovarian reserve, tube health, and a realistic picture of your specific situation.

career_moment.narrative: However you've decided to approach family planning, the career dimension is real and worth planning around — not avoiding.

choice_a.label: Tell me how to time pregnancy planning with my career trajectory
choice_a.consequence: Timing is more plannable than you expected. Identifying the career milestones you want to reach first gives you a concrete planning window.

choice_b.label: Tell me what maternity leave and returning to work looks like practically
choice_b.consequence: The practical realities are more navigable than the silence around them suggests. Women do this regularly and successfully.

default.headline: Career timing and pregnancy — practical planning
default.body: Timing a pregnancy around a promotion, a major project, or a career transition is something women do deliberately and successfully. The most useful planning horizon is 12 to 18 months — identifying what career milestones you want to hit first, working back from a rough target window, and ensuring your health baseline is in order.
default.action: Identify the one or two career milestones you want to reach before a pregnancy. Work backward to a rough target window. Book a pre-conception checkup for 3 to 6 months before that window.

pcos.headline: Career and pregnancy planning with PCOS
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, irregular ovulation means conception may not happen on the precise timeline you plan. Building a slightly wider planning window — 18 to 24 months rather than 12 — accounts for the possibility that ovulation support may be needed without creating urgency.
pcos.action: When planning your career-pregnancy timeline with PCOS, build in a 6-month buffer for the possibility of ovulation support. This is not pessimism — it's realistic planning.

endometriosis.headline: Career and pregnancy planning with endometriosis
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, discussing the timing of any surgical intervention relative to your pregnancy plans is important. Surgery before trying to conceive — to remove endometriomas or improve tube health — may improve your chances but has a recovery period. This conversation is best had with your specialist 12 to 18 months before your target window.
endometriosis.action: Have the timing conversation with your endometriosis specialist 12 to 18 months before your target pregnancy window — surgical timing matters and requires advance planning.

track_family_planning_32_35.title: Family Planning — Extended Options Window
track_family_planning_32_35.framing: At 32 to 35, family planning decisions carry more weight — not because options have disappeared, but because the timeline is shorter and the decisions deserve honest information.

career_moment.narrative: The honest conversation about family planning at 32 to 35 isn't about panic. It's about having real information to make real decisions — with all the options on the table.

choice_a.label: Tell me the honest picture for natural conception at my age
choice_a.consequence: The honest picture is more nuanced than the headlines suggest. Natural conception remains possible for most women at 32 to 35. The odds shift — but they don't disappear.

choice_b.label: Tell me about assisted reproduction options at this stage
choice_b.consequence: IVF, IUI, egg donation — these are real options with real success rates at this age. Understanding them removes the fear and replaces it with information.

default.headline: Natural conception at 32 to 35 — the real numbers
default.body: At 32, roughly 78 percent of women who try for 12 months will conceive. At 35, that figure is approximately 66 percent. These are not catastrophic declines — they are gradual shifts that mean more women at 35 may need a longer runway or some clinical support, but the majority do conceive. The alarmist narrative significantly overstates the case.
default.action: A pre-conception fertility assessment at 32 to 35 — AMH, antral follicle count, and a specialist consultation — gives you your personal picture rather than a statistical average.

pcos.headline: Natural conception and IVF with PCOS at 32 to 35
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, ovulation induction remains the first-line approach at this age. If first-line treatment doesn't result in conception within 3 to 6 cycles, IVF is the next step. IVF with PCOS typically has good outcomes because ovarian response to stimulation is usually strong.
pcos.action: Book a fertility consultation now if you want children and haven't started trying. With PCOS at 32 to 35, the sooner the pathway is established the better — not because it's urgent, but because the steps take time.

endometriosis.headline: Natural conception and IVF with endometriosis at 32 to 35
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, a specialist fertility assessment at this age is important. The impact of endometriosis on fertility is highly variable — some women conceive naturally without difficulty, others need assisted reproduction. Your specific picture — ovarian reserve, tube health, disease severity — determines which pathway is most appropriate.
endometriosis.action: Book a specialist fertility consultation with an endometriosis-experienced fertility doctor. The assessment of your specific situation is more useful than any general statistic.

career_moment.narrative: All the paths are real. Natural conception, assisted reproduction, adoption, child-free living — none of these is a consolation prize. They are all choices that women make deliberately and live fully.

choice_a.label: Tell me about IVF — what it involves and what the realistic outcomes are
choice_a.consequence: IVF is more routine and less dramatic than its cultural representation. The success rates at 32 to 35 are real. The process is demanding but manageable. You understand it now.

choice_b.label: Tell me about adoption and child-free living as equally valid paths
choice_b.consequence: Both paths are complete. Neither is a default. Understanding all the options is what makes any choice feel like a genuine one.

default.headline: IVF at 32 to 35 — realistic success rates
default.body: IVF success rates at 32 to 35 using own eggs are approximately 35 to 40 percent per cycle. This means most cycles do not result in a live birth — but across multiple cycles, cumulative success rates are higher. Age 35 is the threshold above which NICE and ACOG guidelines recommend earlier referral to assisted reproduction. Understanding this means understanding the timeline for decision-making.
default.action: A fertility consultation at 32 to 35 — AMH, antral follicle count, specialist assessment — gives you a personalised success rate estimate rather than a population average.

pcos.headline: IVF with PCOS at 32 to 35 — the realistic picture
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, IVF success rates at this age are generally comparable to or slightly above the average because ovarian response is typically strong. The OHSS risk remains relevant and requires monitoring. A clinic experienced in PCOS management is important at this stage.
pcos.action: When consulting fertility clinics at this age with PCOS, ask specifically for their per-cycle and cumulative success rates for your age group and condition.

endometriosis.headline: IVF with endometriosis at 32 to 35
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, IVF success rates may be affected by ovarian reserve and the impact of the condition on egg quality. Specialist fertility clinics with endometriosis expertise — not just general fertility clinics — are better positioned to optimise your treatment protocol.
endometriosis.action: Ask your fertility clinic specifically about their experience with endometriosis patients and how they adjust their protocol for the condition.

track_child_free.title: Your Health, Your Terms
track_child_free.framing: Reproductive health is not synonymous with fertility. Your health matters on your own terms — for your quality of life, your energy, your long-term wellbeing.

career_moment.narrative: Children are not part of your plan — and that is a complete plan. Your reproductive health still matters entirely on its own terms. Here is what that looks like.

choice_a.label: Tell me about long-term hormonal health without pregnancy
choice_a.consequence: The picture is broader than you expected. Hormonal health affects your energy, mood, bone density, and cardiovascular health — none of it contingent on fertility.

choice_b.label: Tell me what perimenopause looks like and how to prepare
choice_b.consequence: Understanding perimenopause in advance removes the shock. It begins on average in the mid-to-late forties. The symptoms, timelines, and what helps are all knowable and manageable.

default.headline: Reproductive health is not the same as fertility
default.body: PCOS, endometriosis, fibroids, and thyroid conditions affect quality of life, energy, pain levels, and long-term health regardless of whether children are wanted. Managing these conditions matters for you — your daily experience, your cardiovascular health, your bone density, your mental health. None of that is contingent on family planning.
default.action: Annual gynaecology checkup from 25 onward is a standard recommendation for all women — not just those considering pregnancy. Book yours if you haven't had one in the last year.

pcos.headline: PCOS long-term health without fertility focus
pcos.body: Since you have PCOS, the long-term metabolic risks — Type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome — exist independently of fertility intentions. Managing PCOS well is about your quality of life and long-term health. The management approach is exactly the same regardless of family planning status.
pcos.action: Ensure your PCOS management includes annual metabolic monitoring — fasting glucose, insulin, lipid panel, blood pressure. These are the health outcomes that matter regardless of fertility plans.

endometriosis.headline: Endometriosis and quality of life — your health is sufficient reason
endometriosis.body: Since you have endometriosis, your quality of life is a sufficient reason for treatment — you do not need to frame your condition in fertility terms to access care. Pain, fatigue, and the mental health impact of endometriosis all deserve treatment on their own terms.
endometriosis.action: If any clinician frames your endometriosis management primarily in fertility terms when that is not your priority, ask explicitly: what is the best approach for my quality of life specifically?

fibroids.headline: Fibroids without fertility considerations
fibroids.body: Since you have fibroids, management without fertility considerations focuses entirely on symptom control and quality of life. Treatment options that might be deferred for fertility reasons — endometrial ablation, for example — may be entirely appropriate for you. This is worth discussing explicitly with your gynaecologist.
fibroids.action: Tell your gynaecologist explicitly that fertility preservation is not a consideration in your treatment decisions. This may open treatment options that wouldn't otherwise be discussed.

thyroid.headline: Thyroid health long-term — bone and cardiovascular
thyroid.body: Since you have a thyroid condition, thyroid hormones affect bone density and cardiovascular health over the long term. These are reasons to ensure optimal thyroid management that have nothing to do with fertility.
thyroid.action: Ask your GP how your thyroid management accounts for long-term bone density and cardiovascular health, not just TSH normalisation.

iron_deficiency.headline: Iron deficiency without fertility context
iron_deficiency.body: Since you have iron deficiency, managing it well matters for your energy, immune function, cognitive performance, and cardiovascular health — entirely independently of fertility. Treatment options including directly addressing heavy menstrual bleeding are appropriate and worth pursuing.
iron_deficiency.action: Ask your GP for a long-term iron management strategy that addresses the underlying cause, not just supplementation to keep levels acceptable.

calcium_vitamin_d.headline: Calcium and Vitamin D for long-term bone health
calcium_vitamin_d.body: Since you have calcium or Vitamin D deficiency, adequate levels through your thirties and forties protect bone density against the accelerated loss of perimenopause. This is a long-term health investment with a 30-year payoff that has nothing to do with fertility.
calcium_vitamin_d.action: Annual Vitamin D monitoring and consistent calcium intake are your maintenance requirements. Confirm both are in place at your annual health review.

end_screen.title: You made it to the long healthy life.
end_screen.closing_line: Your career and your health are not in competition. They are the same long game — and you have been playing both.
